# Source type: pdf
# Generated by scripts/ingest_materials.py

--- Page 1 ---
Engineering Problem Solving Process
Woojin Park
Life Enhancing Technology Laboratory
SNU Department of Industrial Engineering

--- Page 2 ---
Engineering Problem Solving Process
• Engineering problem solving provides a systematic way to 
generate and implement improvements
• It is especially useful when improving work methods, products, 
services, or systems
• Rather than jumping to one idea, engineers examine multiple 
alternatives and select the best one
• The process is iterative, evidence-based, and improvement-
oriented

--- Page 3 ---
Why a Structured Process Is Needed
• Real-world engineering problems rarely have one obvious 
answer
• It is usually impossible to consider all conceivable alternatives 
at once
• A structured process helps narrow the search space and make 
rational choices
• The current method is also an alternative and should be 
evaluated against other possibilities
• Even an ideal future concept can serve as a useful reference 
point during solution search

--- Page 4 ---
Five-Step Engineering Problem Solving Process (DASSI)
• Define the problem
• Analyze the problem, including data collection
• Search for possible alternatives
• Select the best alternative through comparison
• Implement the solution and follow up

--- Page 5 ---

--- Page 6 ---
Step 1: Define the Problem
• A well-defined problem is already half solved
• Problem definition clarifies what is wrong, for whom, and 
under what conditions
• The key question is not merely “What can we improve?” but 
“What is the actual problem?”
• Poorly defined problems often lead to wasted effort and weak 
solutions
• A vague problem statement should be turned into a specific 
and actionable one

--- Page 7 ---
Why Problem Definition Matters So Much
• Good alternatives are often discovered already in the definition or 
analysis stage
• A problem must be real, meaningful, and worth solving
• There is no perfect work method; almost every system contains 
room for improvement
• Improvement opportunities may emerge because of changes in:
– Quality requirements
– Quantity or demand
– Raw materials
– Equipment
– Time constraints
– Organizational support

--- Page 8 ---
Step 2: Analyze the Problem
• Problem analysis requires careful observation and data 
collection
• The goal is to understand causes, constraints, and current 
performance
• Analysis should distinguish symptoms from root causes
• Data makes the improvement effort more objective and 
credible
• Without analysis, solution ideas are more likely to be based 
on assumptions

--- Page 9 ---
Step 3: Search for Alternatives
• Engineers should generate multiple alternatives before 
selecting one
• At least three alternatives should be explored whenever 
possible
• The existing method should be treated as one alternative
• A highly idealized or future-oriented concept may be another
• Additional alternatives can be generated through 
brainstorming and related ideation methods

--- Page 10 ---
Compare Alternatives and Select the Best One
• Alternatives should be evaluated using explicit criteria
• Typical criteria include:
– Effectiveness
– Cost
– Feasibility
– Safety
– Time
– Compatibility with organizational conditions
• The goal is not to choose the most interesting idea, but the 
most appropriate one
• Selection should be evidence-based rather than intuition-
driven

--- Page 11 ---
Step 5: Implement and Follow Up
• Improvement does not create value until it is implemented
• Follow-up is necessary to confirm whether the solution 
actually works in practice
• Implementation often reveals new issues not visible during 
analysis
• Continuous monitoring helps stabilize the improved system
• A solution is not complete until it is sustained

--- Page 12 ---
Related Improvement Cycles
• The engineering problem-solving process is conceptually 
similar to other improvement frameworks
• Examples include:
– PDSA: Plan–Do–Study–Act
– PDCA: Plan–Do–Check–Act
– DMAIC: Define–Measure–Analyze–Improve–Control
• All of these frameworks emphasize structured improvement, 
evidence, and iteration
• The naming differs, but the core logic is highly similar

--- Page 13 ---

--- Page 14 ---

--- Page 15 ---
Key Principle: A Problem Well Defined Is Half Solved
• “A problem well defined is half solved already”
• “A problem identified is half solved already”
• These statements emphasize the value of disciplined thinking 
before action
• Many failed improvements originate from weak diagnosis 
rather than weak creativity
• Better understanding usually leads to better solutions

--- Page 16 ---
Why Good Ideas Often Fail
• Many good ideas are never adopted
• Technical merit alone does not guarantee implementation
• Resistance to change is one of the biggest barriers to 
improvement
• In practice, an organization may reject useful ideas for social 
or emotional reasons
• Engineering improvement therefore involves both technical 
and human challenges

--- Page 17 ---
Resistance to Change: Why It Happens
• People tend to prefer familiar methods and routines
• A new idea may be interpreted as criticism of the current 
method
• People may resist ideas proposed by others, especially 
outsiders
• Fear is often involved:
– Fear of failure
– Fear of loss of status
– Fear of disruption
– Fear of embarrassment
• Past frustration may also matter if earlier good suggestions 
were ignored

--- Page 18 ---
How to Reduce Resistance to Change
• Reduce fear by communicating clearly and respectfully
• Build teamwork around the improvement effort
• Involve the people affected by the change
• Create trust before demanding adoption
• People are more likely to accept change when they feel 
respected and included
• Implementation is easier when improvement is seen as 
shared progress rather than imposed criticism

--- Page 19 ---
Other Barriers to Adoption
• Even beyond psychological resistance, practical barriers can 
block implementation
• Important considerations include:
– Risk factors
– Training needs
– Initial trial and startup issues
– Maintenance and repair issues
– Timing
– Safety
– Company policy
• A technically good solution may still fail if these realities are 
ignored

--- Page 20 ---
Final Takeaways
• Engineering problem solving is systematic rather than 
accidental
• Good improvement begins with clear problem definition and 
careful analysis
• Multiple alternatives should be generated and compared
• Implementation and follow-up are essential parts of the 
process
• Human factors, especially resistance to change, must be 
managed along with technical factors
• Effective improvement requires both sound logic and 
organizational sensitivity
